title: A Modest Proposal: For preventing the children of poor people in Ireland, from being a burden on their parents or country, and for making them beneficial to the publick
author: Swift, Jonathan
summary: Swift's narrator offers a savage satirical proposal: to solve Ireland's poverty by selling and consuming poor infants. He computes numbers, recommends reserving some for breeding, fattening year-old children for market, and suggests culinary preparations, skins for gloves, and tavern demand. The scheme supposedly reduces parish burdens, increases landlords' incomes, discourages abortions, and diminishes Roman Catholic influence. He cites informants — an American, a Cavan gentleman, merchants, and a Formosan tale — and mock-justifies cruelty by invented economic advantages. The essay exposes British indifference, class exploitation, and the brutality of political rhetoric through corrosive irony and calculated figures and bitter satire.

characters.name: Narrator / Proposer (first-person 'I')
characters.lifespan: remains the voice of the essay; proposes selling infants as a satirical indictment of policies

characters.name: A very knowing American (of the narrator's acquaintance in London)
characters.lifespan: cited as source for culinary claims about infants; used to buttress the narrator's satire

characters.name: Principal gentleman (of the county of Cavan)
characters.lifespan: quoted by narrator about learning to steal; serves as an anecdotal authority

characters.name: Our merchants
characters.lifespan: referenced for being aware of child 'value' on the exchange; part of the satire on commerce

characters.name: A very worthy person, a true lover of his country (patriot)
characters.personality: pretended patriot; offers a refinement to the scheme
characters.lifespan: suggests selling older youths; used to amplify the narrator's ironic claims

characters.name: His friend
characters.lifespan: relates the Formosan anecdote; helps trace the idea's supposed origin

characters.name: Psalmanaazor (native of Formosa)
characters.lifespan: described as having related a tale of selling executed bodies in Formosa; used by narrator as an odd corroboration

characters.name: The Emperor (referenced in the Formosan anecdote)
characters.lifespan: mentioned only in the anecdote about imperial consumption of bodies

characters.name: Imperial majesty's prime minister (referenced)
characters.lifespan: appears only in the Formosan anecdote

characters.name: Squire (landlord, class example)
characters.lifespan: portrayed as beneficiary of the scheme; emblematic of landlord greed

characters.name: Mothers / Beggars (female sex)
characters.lifespan: portrayed as impoverished and compelled to sell infants; used to dramatize social misery

characters.name: Poor children / infants
characters.lifespan: proposed to be sold and eaten at one year old; central victims of the satire

characters.name: Papists / Roman Catholics (Popish community)
characters.personality: described as numerous and politically suspect by narrator
characters.lifespan: targeted rhetorically; suggested the scheme would lessen their numbers

characters.name: Young labourers
characters.lifespan: depicted as starving or dying from want; used to underline social neglect

unusual_words.definition: deep sadness or gloom
unusual_words.context: Opening: 'It is a melancholy object to those...'

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of cabin doors or small house doors
unusual_words.context: Describes streets and 'cabbin-doors' crowded with beggars

unusual_words.definition: archaic form of strolling (wandering, roaming)
unusual_words.context: Mothers 'are forced to employ all their time in stroling to beg sustenance'

unusual_words.definition: persistently begging or entreating
unusual_words.context: Children 'importuning every passenger for an alms'

unusual_words.definition: charitable gifts given to the poor
unusual_words.context: Passengers asked 'for an alms' in the streets

unusual_words.definition: extraordinary in size or extent
unusual_words.context: Refers to 'a prodigious number of children' as a grievance

unusual_words.definition: one who preserves or saves
unusual_words.context: Would 'deserve ... to have his statue set up for a preserver of the nation'

unusual_words.definition: those who propose schemes or plans
unusual_words.context: The narrator criticizes 'the several schemes of our projectors'

unusual_words.definition: relating to the sun; here meaning a year
unusual_words.context: A child 'may be supported by her milk, for a solar year'

unusual_words.definition: archaic past participle of drop; newly born
unusual_words.context: A child 'just dropt from its dam'

unusual_words.definition: mother (of an animal or, used here, metaphorically of a child)
unusual_words.context: A child 'just dropt from its dam' is supported on its mother's milk

unusual_words.definition: nourishing substances or food
unusual_words.context: Costs 'at most not above the value of two shillings' in nutriments

unusual_words.definition: archaic form of 'increases'
unusual_words.context: A child 'in a solar year, if tolerably nursed, encreaseth to 28 pounds'

unusual_words.definition: those on trial or under probation
unusual_words.context: Children 'can however be properly looked upon only as probationers'

unusual_words.definition: having promise or being forward in development
unusual_words.context: Some are 'of towardly parts' for learning skills early

unusual_words.definition: able to be sold; marketable
unusual_words.context: A child before twelve 'is no saleable commodity'

unusual_words.definition: archaic variant of ragout, a meat stew
unusual_words.context: The narrator: 'it will equally serve in a fricasee, or a ragoust'

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of fricassee, a stewed or fried dish
unusual_words.context: Child 'will equally serve in a fricasee'

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of increased
unusual_words.context: The nation's stock 'will be thereby encreased fifty thousand pounds'

unusual_words.definition: derogatory term for Roman Catholic
unusual_words.context: Refers to 'Popish infants' being more plentiful

unusual_words.definition: title for a claimant to a throne (here the Jacobite claimant)
unusual_words.context: Some children 'leave their... country, to fight for the Pretender in Spain'

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of Barbados (Caribbean island)
unusual_words.context: Children 'sell themselves to the Barbadoes'

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of public
unusual_words.context: Used in full title and throughout: 'publick consideration' or 'publick advantage'

unusual_words.definition: a local church district responsible for poor relief
unusual_words.context: Children would no longer be a charge 'upon their parents, or the parish'

unusual_words.definition: people living in small rural cottages; smallholders
unusual_words.context: Includes 'all cottagers, labourers, and four-fifths of the farmers' as beggars

unusual_words.definition: physically injured or crippled
unusual_words.context: Worries about 'poor people, who are aged, diseased, or maimed'

unusual_words.definition: archaic form of 'incumbrance', meaning burden
unusual_words.context: Refers to the aged and maimed as a grievous 'incumbrance'

unusual_words.definition: decaying, decomposition
unusual_words.context: The aged are 'every day dying, and rotting, by cold and famine'

unusual_words.definition: noxious parasites or pests
unusual_words.context: People die 'by cold and famine, and filth, and vermin'

unusual_words.definition: young unmarried women
unusual_words.context: Suggestion to sell 'young lads and maidens, not exceeding fourteen years'

unusual_words.definition: well-rounded, fat and fleshy
unusual_words.context: Describes a 'plump girl of fifteen' in the Formosan anecdote

unusual_words.definition: high-ranking officials in imperial China (used in anecdote)
unusual_words.context: The carcass in the anecdote was sold 'to his imperial majesty’s prime minister of state, and other great mandarins'

unusual_words.definition: executed by being nailed to a cross
unusual_words.context: A plump girl 'who was crucified for an attempt to poison the Emperor'

unusual_words.definition: a gallows-like structure for public execution/display
unusual_words.context: Bodies sold 'in joints from the gibbet'

unusual_words.definition: the dead body of an animal or person (used provocatively)
unusual_words.context: Repeated references to the 'carcass' of children as marketable goods

unusual_words.definition: butchers' market or slaughterhouse
unusual_words.context: Proposes 'shambles may be appointed' in Dublin for selling infants

unusual_words.definition: wine sellers; tavern keepers
unusual_words.context: This food 'would likewise bring great custom to taverns, where the vintners will... procure the best receipts'

unusual_words.definition: competitive striving to equal or excel
unusual_words.context: Predicts 'an honest emulation among the married women' to produce the fattest child

unusual_words.definition: to give birth to pigs; used metaphorically for animals birthing
unusual_words.context: Men would care for wives 'as they are now of their mares in foal, their cows in calf, or sows when they are ready to farrow'

unusual_words.definition: adult female pigs
unusual_words.context: Used in simile comparing care for pregnant women to animals: 'sows when they are ready to farrow'

unusual_words.definition: means of supporting life; earning a living
unusual_words.context: Mothers unable 'to work for their honest livelihood' and forced to beg

unusual_words.definition: food or provision sustaining life
unusual_words.context: Mothers 'stroling to beg sustenance for their helpless infants'

unusual_words.definition: openly declared or avowed
unusual_words.context: Not limited 'only to the children of professed beggars'

unusual_words.definition: former unit of British currency
unusual_words.context: Costs 'at most not above the value of two shillings' to support a baby by milk

unusual_words.definition: leftover bits of food
unusual_words.context: Mother may get 'two shillings, which the mother may certainly get, or the value in scraps'

unusual_words.definition: to have a miscarriage; lose a pregnancy
unusual_words.context: Subtracts fifty thousand for women 'who miscarry, or whose children die...'

unusual_words.definition: traders or businessmen
unusual_words.context: Merchants assert a child 'before twelve years old, is no saleable commodity'

unusual_words.definition: cooked slowly in liquid
unusual_words.context: A child 'is, at a year old, a most delicious nourishing and wholesome food, whether stewed, roasted...'

unusual_words.definition: a region or a quarter of an animal (fore or hind quarter)
unusual_words.context: A 'fore or hind quarter will make a reasonable dish'

unusual_words.definition: an average or middle estimate
unusual_words.context: Narrator 'has reckoned upon a medium' for child weight

unusual_words.definition: clear, exact; here meaning net or clean profit
unusual_words.context: Mother will have 'eight shillings neat profit'

unusual_words.definition: to strip off the skin
unusual_words.context: Those thrifty 'may flay the carcass; the skin... will make admirable gloves'

unusual_words.definition: having moral qualms or conscience
unusual_words.context: Some 'scrupulous people might be apt to censure such a practice... as bordering upon cruelty'

unusual_words.definition: proper name used in the anecdote (Formosan native)
unusual_words.context: Cited as 'the famous Psalmanaazor, a native of the island Formosa'

unusual_words.context: Psalmanaazor is described as 'a native of the island Formosa'

unusual_words.definition: an old small-denomination coin
unusual_words.context: Describes girls 'without one single groat to their fortunes'

unusual_words.definition: here means a sedan-chair used for personal transport
unusual_words.context: Cannot 'stir abroad without a chair' describing poor fashion-conscious people

unusual_words.context: Appear 'at a playhouse and assemblies in foreign fineries'

unusual_words.definition: fine or showy dress and ornament
unusual_words.context: People appearing 'in foreign fineries which they never will pay for'

unusual_words.definition: in low spirits from loss of hope
unusual_words.context: Some 'persons of a desponding spirit are in great concern' about the poor aged

unusual_words.definition: ill or afflicted with disease
unusual_words.context: Concerns 'poor people, who are aged, diseased, or maimed'

unusual_words.definition: year (Latin)

unusual_words.definition: festive, jovial

unusual_words.definition: baptismal ceremonies
unusual_words.context: Listed as occasions where the meat might be served

unusual_words.definition: famous or well known

unusual_words.context: Merchants assert that 'before twelve years old, is no saleable commodity'

unusual_words.context: This food 'would likewise bring great custom to taverns'

unusual_words.definition: archaic term for recipes or instructions for cooking

unusual_words.definition: having skill or expertise
unusual_words.context: A 'skilful cook' could make the meat as expensive as desired

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of diet
unusual_words.context: Says 'fish being a prolifick dyet' resulting in more births

unusual_words.definition: relating to Rome; used with Catholick
unusual_words.context: Refers to 'Roman Catholick countries' in demographic claim

unusual_words.definition: archaic spelling of Catholic
unusual_words.context: Used in phrase 'Roman Catholick countries' and 'Popish infants'

unusual_words.definition: moral sense of right and wrong
unusual_words.context: People 'leave their country, than stay at home and pay tithes against their conscience'

unusual_words.definition: members of non-Catholic Christian churches
